title: Australia's ABC Staff Announce Historic 24-Hour Strike Over Pay and AI Use
description: Australian Broadcasting Corporation staff are set to launch their first strike in 20 years, disrupting live TV and radio following a rejected pay offer and unresolved concerns regarding artificial intelligence.
lede: For the first time in two decades, journalists and staff at the Australian Broadcasting Corporation (ABC) will walk off the job on Wednesday in a historic 24-hour strike. The unprecedented industrial action comes after a staggering 60% of employees decisively rejected management's 10% pay rise offer, setting the stage for major disruptions across live television and radio broadcasts nationwide.

sections.heading: What Happened
paragraphs: Staff at the Australian Broadcasting Corporation (ABC) will go on strike on Wednesday for the first time in 20 years, escalating a bitter dispute over pay and working conditions. The 24-hour strike will commence at 11:00 local time (00:00 GMT) and is anticipated to cause widespread disruptions to the network's live television and radio operations.

The strike authorization follows a decisive vote where 60% of ABC staff rejected a proposed 10% total pay increase distributed over three years. Management's offer consisted of a 3.5% bump in the first year, followed by 3.25% increases in the subsequent two years.

With 75% of the network's roughly 4,500 staff members participating in Sunday's vote, union representatives declared the offer fundamentally inadequate. Michael Slezak, representing the Media, Entertainment and Arts Alliance (MEAA), stated unequivocally that workers cannot accept a deal that effectively "sends pay backwards against inflation."

sections.heading: Why It Matters
paragraphs: The dispute exposes a widening rift between media executives and the workforce over the fundamental value of journalism in a high-inflation environment. Australia recorded an annual inflation rate of 3.8% in January, rendering the proposed pay increases a de facto pay cut in real terms for many ABC employees.

Beyond base compensation, the negotiations have stalled over critical modern workplace issues. Unions highlighted that management's proposal failed to address essential demands, including provisions for reproductive health leave and, critically, binding protections regarding the implementation of artificial intelligence.

sections.heading: The Bigger Picture
paragraphs: The inclusion of AI protections in the unions' demands underscores a growing, global existential anxiety within the media industry. Journalists are increasingly demanding contractual safeguards against automation, with MEAA's Slezak pointing out management's refusal "to rule out replacing ABC journalists with AI bots."

This marks the first time ABC staff have engaged in strike action since 2006, signaling a return to militant industrial tactics not seen in a generation at the public broadcaster. ABC managing director Hugh Marks defended the rejected offer as "the maximum level the ABC can sustainably provide," claiming it was both financially responsible and balanced.

sections.heading: What's Next
paragraphs: ABC audiences should brace for immediate consequences, with Jocelyn Gammie of the Community and Public Sector Union (CPSU) warning that disruptions are "inevitable" unless a "fair offer" is tabled immediately.

As the Wednesday deadline looms, the ABC has announced intentions to ask the Fair Work Commission, Australia's workplace tribunal, to intervene. The tribunal is expected to facilitate last-minute arbitration in an attempt to avert the unprecedented blackout.
